title: Senate Bill 1728 Fully Affirms Legal Abortion
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman argues that Oklahoma's SB1728 (the 'Unborn Person Wrongful Death Act') is yet another incremental pro-life bill that regulates abortion rather than abolishing it, serving primarily to give complicit senators anti-abortion credibility while they continue to block the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act (SB13).

questions.question: What does SB1728 actually do, and why does Silberman say it does not abolish abortion?
questions.answer: SB1728 only allows the mother or grandparents of a murdered preborn child to sue an abortionist who violates existing abortion regulations (such as parental consent documentation, informed consent, or screening for impaired judgement). It treats abortion itself as legal and merely adds a regulatory checklist for abortionists to satisfy.
questions.quote: SB1728 allows the mother who had the abortion or the grandparents of the murdered child to sue the abortionist if the abortionist violates an abortion regulation; for instance if the abortionist fails to document parental consent or judicial bypass before murdering the child of a minor, if the abortionist fails to obtain and document compliance with informed consent regulations, or if the abortionist fails to document that they screened the mother for impaired judgement, among a few other regulatory violations.

questions.question: Why does Silberman call SB1728's use of 'unborn person' language schizophrenic?
questions.answer: Because the bill rhetorically affirms personhood by name while legally treating preborn children as something less than persons. A bill that genuinely treated unborn children as persons would extend equal protection to them, not provide a procedural checklist for their lawful killing.
questions.quote: Senate Bill 1728 fully affirms the legality of the practice of murdering unborn children. It simply provides the abortionist with a few hoops to jump through to make the unborn person’s death rightful.

questions.question: What did Senator Bullard admit on the Senate floor about his own bill?
questions.answer: Under cross-examination by Senator Joseph Silk on March 9th, Bullard conceded that the bill does nothing more than impose a checklist on abortionists, and went further to claim that this kind of checklist is the most effective way to protect preborn children.
questions.quote: Bullard said not only is providing a checklist for abortionists all his bill does, but further, he admitted his belief that providing abortionists a pre-murder checklist is the best possible way to protect preborn children.

questions.question: What does Silberman say is the actually effective alternative to SB1728?
questions.answer: Writing a law that treats unborn human beings as fully equal to born human beings. Legislators are obligated as God's deacons to write just laws and uphold justice even when superiors pressure them otherwise; the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act has done exactly this since 2016 but has been killed every year.
questions.quote: The best thing to do to protect unborn children is to write a law treating unborn human beings as fully equal to born human beings. Senator Bullard and his colleagues are legislators. Their job is to be God’s deacon of wrath against the evil-doer. Their job is to write just laws.

questions.question: What evidence does the article give that incremental regulations are not even reducing abortion?
questions.answer: Despite a steady stream of regulatory bills passed every session, abortions have actually increased in Oklahoma in recent years, undermining the claim that incrementalism is achieving even its modest stated goals.
questions.quote: These bills don’t even accomplish what we’re told they accomplish. Abortions have [increased in Oklahoma](https://lozierinstitute.org/abortion-reporting-oklahoma-2017/) in recent years despite the abortion regulations passed every session!

questions.question: How does Silberman use a hypothetical about brown-haired 25-year-olds to frame the love-of-neighbor obligation?
questions.answer: He argues that if SCOTUS declared it legal to murder a class of adults, no one would respond by lobbying for regulatory tweaks; they would demand outright criminalization. Loving the preborn as oneself therefore demands the same response: total and immediate abolition, not regulation.
questions.quote: If SCOTUS opined that it was legal to murder brown-haired 25-year-olds, I assure you I would not ask the Oklahoma State Government to fiddle with regulatory codes. I would demand with every bit of passion within me that the State of Oklahoma make it illegal to murder me in defiance of that supreme court opinion. To do anything less for preborn human beings would thus be a failure to love my neighbor as myself.

questions.question: Why did Bullard, Allen, and Murdock turn against SB13 after previously supporting abolition work?
questions.answer: Silberman attributes the reversal to pressure from Senate Pro-Tem Greg Treat, who had already punished Senator Silk for publicly campaigning for abolition. He suggests Treat likely threatened the three senators' committee leadership assignments, though he acknowledges this cannot be confirmed.
questions.quote: But pressure from Senate Pro-Tem Greg Treat caused Bullard, Senator Mark Allen, and Senator Casey Murdock to stop supporting abortion’s immediate abolition. Senator Silk was punished by Treat for publicly campaigning for abortion’s abolition, so it’s conceivable that Treat threatened to take committee leadership assignments from Bullard, Allen, and Murdock too unless they stopped supporting the bill.

questions.question: How is Senator Larry Boggs cited as an example of incrementalism being used as electoral cover?
questions.answer: Boggs, facing an abolitionist primary challenger in Warren Hamilton, has become a chief champion of SB1728 to rebuild anti-abortion credibility he lost by refusing to back SB13. He has even smeared abolitionist organizations as 'wolves in sheep's clothing' for pushing past pre-murder checklists.
questions.quote: Boggs [regularly posts](https://www.facebook.com/boggsforsenate/posts/3446068382110951?__xts__[0]=68.ARBQ2wkQxD5lAgjl_korOpwNvhrTsG685SKle2HbufIQ1cZelYQMymE8ooAUd6qnqJGfsJF53b3W-8GcmT6miiJvkEflCOaUFXQQ28-ShPZR1fxPJQVZTcgrEPkuxpSXJB5Q1hhTxe6Zg-R_SK6IDq5cbCE4xZghNKGEQq0BI8d6aYw-JAKDDOOWEkXxMIGSOfPgMwK3pF9hasYZcm264U919hKKXazzzY1bEXWBJmlG6oeeQh6VbIeHZfaf00c7b5kEiBy332jPiA7dfWnY8FDxr2AeBWttGZMNInGjQinR5duSsNMlWXP-&__tn__=H-R) about the progress of SB1728 and has even called abolitionist organizations “wolves in sheep’s clothing” for calling state Senators to move beyond pro-life compromises and pre-murder checklists to actually abolish abortion.

questions.question: How does Silberman use William Lloyd Garrison to frame the moral status of compromise bills?
questions.answer: He invokes Garrison's 1832 condemnation of any scheme presented as a substitute for the immediate and total abolition of slavery, applying that same judgement to SB1728: regardless of intent, a substitute for abolition is itself delusive, cruel, and dangerous because it delays the abolition of abortion.
questions.quote: We regard as delusive, cruel and dangerous, any scheme of expatriation which pretends to aid, either directly or indirectly in the emancipation of the slaves, **or to be a substitute for the immediate and total abolition of slavery**

questions.question: What does Silberman call activists and Christians to do instead of supporting bills like SB1728?
questions.answer: Stop tolerating compromise from pro-life politicians and lobbyists, treat Roe as the legal nullity it is, and demand total and immediate abolition grounded in the equal-protection guarantees of the U.S. and Oklahoma Constitutions and the word of God.
questions.quote: It’s time to stand firmly on the word of God, as well as the U.S. and Oklahoma Constitutions which demand equal protection of the law for all. It’s time to treat Roe as the crime against humanity and legal nullity that it is by abolishing abortion.

related.title: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
related.reason: Lays out the positive case for the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act (SB13) that Silberman says SB1728 is a substitute for.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Names the same Oklahoma pro-life leadership dynamic Silberman blames for killing SB13 in favor of bills like SB1728.

related.title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
related.reason: Directly profiles Greg Treat, whom Silberman identifies as the Senate pressure source that turned Bullard, Allen, and Murdock against abolition.

related.title: Free the States Memo to OK Senators on SB 1728
related.reason: Companion advocacy memo from Free the States addressed to the same SB1728 vote this article opposes.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Generalizes the anti-incrementalism argument Silberman makes specifically about SB1728.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual, Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Develops the Garrisonian 'immediate and total abolition' framing Silberman quotes against substitutes for abolition.

related.title: Anti-SB13 Campaigners Aren't Ignorant. They're Trying to Deceive You.
related.reason: Documents the same pattern of Oklahoma operatives using pro-life branding to block SB13 that Silberman accuses Boggs of.
